metadata.title: Questions and Answer on Guideline: Clinical Investigation of Corticosteroids Intended for Use on the Skin

rules.rule_text: A vasoconstriction assay (VCA) can replace clinical efficacy data only if the generic medicinal product has the same or a similar qualitative and quantitative composition (excipients) as the reference product. Qualitative changes in composition require clinical efficacy studies.
rules.verbatim_quote: a pharmacodynamic model, e.g. the vasoconstriction assay (VCA) is sufficient only if the generic medicinal product possesses the same or a similar qualitative and quantitative composition to that of the reference product... qualitative changes in the composition imply the need for clinical efficacy data.
rules.exceptions: Minor quantitative changes in the same excipients may allow VCA instead of clinical efficacy studies

rules.rule_text: A VCA-based abridged application cannot be used to compare two different steroid concentrations or two different pharmaceutical forms (e.g., cream vs ointment), as potency classification of the corticosteroid alone cannot predict efficacy of the entire preparation.
rules.verbatim_quote: it is not possible to present an abridged application for a topical corticosteroid preparation based on a VCA between two different steroid concentrations or two different pharmaceutical forms, e.g. cream and ointment, as the potency of the corticosteroid alone (classification system to assess the strength of the corticosteroid) is not able to predict the efficacy of the entire preparation.

rules.rule_text: VCA pivotal studies require a minimum of 12 healthy subjects with adequate vasoconstriction response to topical corticosteroids. Reference products must include different potency classes (equally potent, more potent, less potent) and must have received a Marketing Authorisation based on a full dossier.
rules.verbatim_quote: For the pivotal study a minimum of 12 healthy persons have to be included... The reference products should include different potency classes (suggested equally potent, more potent and less potent) and have received a Marketing Authorisation based on a full dossier.
cross_references: FDA Guidance for Industry 'topical dermatologic corticosteroids, in vivo bioequivalence', June 1995

rules.rule_text: Test product, vehicle, reference product(s), and untreated control must be randomly assigned to application sites on the ventral forearms.
rules.verbatim_quote: Test product, vehicle, reference product(s), and untreated control should be randomly assigned to application sites on the ventral forearms.

rules.rule_text: VCA reaction must be determined at baseline (before application), at time of drug product removal, and at several time points after removal (e.g., 2, 4, 6, 19, 24 hours). The time course must be followed until return to baseline. A single time point estimation is not acceptable.
rules.verbatim_quote: The vasoconstriction reaction should be determined at baseline (before drug application), time of drug product removal and several times after drug product removal (e.g. 2, 4, 6, 19, 24 hours). The time course of response should be followed until return to baseline to ensure that the maximal pharmacodynamic response is observed... A single time point for estimation of the vasoconstriction reaction is not acceptable.

rules.rule_text: The VCA assay must be optimised to compare products in the linear portion of the blanching curve. Several application times must be tested in pre-test. The lower limit of sensitivity must also be determined. AUC data must be generated from multiple time points.
rules.verbatim_quote: The assay must be optimised to ensure that the products are compared in the linear portion of the blanching curve. Several application times should be tested in pre-test. Also the lower limit of sensitivity has to be determined. The vasoconstriction reaction should be determined at several time points and AUC data should be generated.

rules.rule_text: Vasoconstriction response measurement should preferably be performed by chromameter, as it possesses greater sensitivity to skin blanching than visual estimation. Digital imaging analysis requires further refinement before acceptance.
rules.source_section: 3. FAQ - Question 2 Answer - Assessment of the vasoconstriction response
rules.verbatim_quote: The measurement of the vasoconstriction response should be preferably performed by chromameter, as the chromameter possesses greater sensitivity to skin blanching than visual estimation does... evaluators should continue to use the visual and chromameter-based methods of comparison of topical corticosteroids.
rules.exceptions: Digital imaging may become acceptable in future after further refinement and validation against human eye assessment

rules.rule_text: When VCA data are available but clinical efficacy data are not, local tolerance studies are usually required. The preferred design is a double-blind vehicle-controlled repeated application study in healthy volunteers.
rules.verbatim_quote: Local tolerance studies are usually required. The preferred design is a double-blind vehicle controlled repeated application study in healthy volunteers.

rules.rule_text: Local tolerance studies must apply both corticosteroid and vehicle on healthy skin under occlusion, 3 times a week for 2 to 3 weeks (depending on recommended treatment duration). Irritation must be graded on a 5-point scale at end of trial.
rules.verbatim_quote: Both corticosteroid and its vehicle are applied on healthy skin under occlusion (to increase possible irritation/insult) 3 times a week for 2 to 3 weeks (study duration will depend on the recommended duration of treatment). Irritation is graded on 5-point scale at the end of the trial.

rules.rule_text: If there is reason to believe that tolerability on diseased skin may be altered by minor changes in the test product (e.g., changing physical consistency of cream or ointment), additional tolerability studies in patients may be necessary.
rules.verbatim_quote: If there is reason to believe that tolerability on diseased skin may be altered even by the minor changes of the test product, e.g. by changing physical consistency of a cream or ointment, additional tolerability studies in patients may be necessary.
rules.exceptions: Only when there is specific reason to suspect altered tolerability
